sentences.text: I [INAUDIBLE].
Well, I was-- my father had encouraged me for sports.
He was very sports-minded.
And then my father would-- my father would take me skating.
My mother always stayed home and watched the store and the business.
But my father liked to go out.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so he would take me for swimming and-- and things like that.
As a matter of fact, yes.
Because I made friends.
They were-- the Maccabi, they had their office in our neighborhood.
And I would go there.
And I got to know them.
And they would take me along for skating and for hiking and things like that.
So I got involved.
And I had a real good time.
Well, it's-- it's hard to say.
Of course it changed when my-- after my father passed away, and then it was a different story.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And I-- but it didn't change drastically.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I used to go off for weekends to the chalet.
We used to go.
Yeah, but not close.
They were sports friends, sports friends.
And, again, as I said and when they went to-- to chalet and things like that, I went always  in a group.
Not enough.
We didn't know enough.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we never thought that there will be a war.
We never thought.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were confident that the-- the Germans will be-- not succeed.
That they won't succeed.
There were refugees, they came from the east, and part from the Ukraine.
And that's where they suffer.
And that's where-- but not-- not from Germany, not from there.
Not from this side.
That was a sad story.
That's a different story.
Again, with this refugees that I'm talking about, because they were the ones that came from the Ukraine.
And they had absolutely nothing.
And they left everything behind.
I guess they didn't have anything even there.
And what they did, they were beggars.
They just came-- went to town and begging.
And some, they had the children, and the-- and in their arms, the babies.
And with the babies they went from door to door for-- for money.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And then they- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] then they-- but then they set up-- oh, how shall I say?
They-- that they should-- the Jewish community looked to it that this-- because there was young and men and girls, and they had no education and nothing.
And so they looked at it, they should be educated and learn something.
And they went and they took the young boys, like the man that was in the building business, he would take one of the other men and teach him to be a builder.
And then there was the girls, they put into-- to people who had to-- were making hats, sewing, to teach them what to do and learn something.
And then they prepared them to-- for Israel.
Well, I was working in the store, I went to work in the store, and I still had private lessons to learn and prepare myself for exam.
Yeah.
Well, what shall I tell you?
I married a very nice and well-educated young man.
And he-- I was introduced to him through a-- through a businessman that-- who used to come to-- he was a-- he used to come to us to our office every so often.
And he introduced me to his friend.
Who?
They are different-- was his name of the friend was Bachmer.
But-- the-- the name of the-- that was [NON-ENGLISH], And-- but he wasn't from-- from my hometown.
So my mother and I-- I had a date with him, but my cousin, my older cousin was with me.
I wouldn't go alone to-- to the date.
[LAUGHS] She is my chaperone.
And-- and he took a liking to me.
And want, you know, wanted to take me out.
And asked me to come to Krakow to there see.
But before I got too close to him, my mother wanted to know who he is and where the family is.
[LAUGHS] So she asked, I mentioned it before, she asked the neighbor about the family who had relatives in Krakow.
And she inquired and she said, the best information she got.
And my-- she encouraged my mother very much that she should let me go out with him and-- and marry him.
And that's what happened.
In my hometown.
I was married in my hometown.
Pardon?
Yeah.
It was a small wedding.
It wasn't a big wedding.
At that time, my father wasn't here anymore.
And it was at home-- the wedding was at home.
No-- no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big fuss or so.
Just the closest relatives came and we had dinner in the house, in our place.
In Krakow.
We lived in Krakow because my husband was an-- he was an architect.
But besides that, he-- he worked and they combined it with a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the lumber business.
Because his father passed away, and he took over the business-- his father's business, too.
So we had to be in Krakow.
We lived in Krakow.
I think it was Kalwaryjska-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] on 12C.
[LAUGHS]
And that was a-- that was a-- and we had an apartment in a new block there.
And one-- yeah, that was after the German took over.
Someone come to the door, without knocking, anything, just walking in, and looking around.
He needed an apartment.
And he says, oh, that is a nice apartment, we have everything new, furnished in the new style.
It was everything.
So he said, oh, I like that.
I'll take it.
And he took-- he liked our bedroom.
He took our bedroom because our bedroom was too modernized.
It had new beds-- the Swedish style, at that time.
Anyhow, he liked it, and he moved in-- without asking or anything.
And he was a German guy who-- he was not even a soldier.
He was-- he-- he was a-- worked in a-- in a repair shop for cars, for autos.
Nothing with the army.
But he was a German, he could do whatever he wanted.
But he was German.
And, yeah, I took my mother when the Germans advanced.
Then I right away called my mother and took her to Krakow, that she should come be with me.
And my mother was very nice to him and tried to keep him happy, you know.
[LAUGHS] And she could speak German with him.
And he would always say, every so often say, we don't mean Jews like you-- we mean the other ones.
Hibbener-- Rudolph Hibbener.
I never thought I would remember.
He only moved into the one bedroom.
And we had the living room, and we had the kitchen, a big-sized kitchen.
And-- and next to the kitchen in those times there was-- there was a room for a maid next to the kitchen.
Because people who could afford as big as that, they had maids.
And there was a room in the kitchen.
So, and that's where-- yeah, and then there was a veranda there in the back somewhere.
Where there was a room-- there was a room then for people when they-- when they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do they do?
How do I say that?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Make Judenrein-- they wanted to make Judenrein.
And the people had to move out and make a ghetto.
They had to move out from the-- from their apartment or wherever they lived there.
And they made a ghetto in Krakow.
Pardon?
So anyhow, why did I talk about the ghetto?
Did--
The German didn't-- they made a ghetto, and we had to get out.
And then we were in the ghetto until they took us to Plaszow-Krakow to the concentration camp.
First they took us to a ghetto, and then from the ghetto--
That's another story.
Because we wanted to take some things.
And this German guy there that we were very friendly with, and they said my mother was nice to him and tried doing it.
He wouldn't let us take anything.
He said, he's sorry-- he can't let us anything take because it's not allowed.
He can do it, it's against the law.
So what we had to do, whatever we want to take in bags, we put it in bags and carried it out in bags, our personal belongings and so.
When you were moved to the ghetto, what was your living space like?
Did you move to an apartment?
Yeah.
We were lucky because they-- they were from Vancouver.
And we knew a lot of people.
So they knew somewhere my husband-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people who left.
There are many people who, when the Germans advanced, they would leave.
And they left behind their big apartment and stuff.
So that's were we could take over.
But it was an apartment.
But the apartment was like that-- in one room, one bedroom, there was-- I was with my husband.
And there was a huge living room.
We had to divide in two.
And then one side-- with closets, they would divide it.
And on one side, one-- my husband's sister lived in one side.
And on the other side, my-- the other sister with her husband lived.
Erika and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forget the other's name.
I'll think of it later.
And that was the other sister's names.
And then we had our mothers.
We had our mothers, so my mother slept in the-- in the kitchen.
And another mother slept in the-- in the room where the help was, you know?
In that room where the help used to be.
So there was-- so that's why we-- had-- you know?
How many people we were?
We were two, and they were three and three-- six.
And our two mothers.
We were all in one room, in one apartment.
Did you work in the ghetto?
No.
No, not really.
My husband still-- my husband still worked and my brother-in-law, he still worked.
They-- they worked, yeah.
But it was very important to work, like, for my husband.
Even said that a German came in to, and he was in the-- in the lumber business.
So he liked, that was one of the big stores-- you don't say stores.
Well, anyhow, he went there.
And, of course, they wanted him to work.
And he did-- not for money, but it was very important that you have a paper that you are working for a Germany company, that became a German company.
So when he went out for work and so the police, the Germans would come and take him and take him away.
And sometimes, you never saw them again.
But this way when he had the paper from a German company, they will leave him alone because it was important that he worked for German.
You know it was interesting.
You know what they-- in the ghetto then, in the ghetto then, somehow times became kind of normal with the time, you know?
We got to used to it.
And became normal.
And there was some, they are still-- they still had money, or they still money-- or they made money or somehow.
And we thought there were ghettos before in-- in the Middle Ages or somewhere, we heard about ghettos.
So we had ghetto there.
You know?
And since there were a lot of people who needed help, so what we did the community, the Jewish community there, they [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] tried to help them and collect money.
And another way of collecting money for these people was Sundays, they made teas, afternoon teas.

So the people who were-- people who were there-- who were there, they still worked, they they still could afford-- they would bring and make tea and coffee and would bake-- they would bake cakes and bring them a cake.
Or make-- or make [NON-ENGLISH] salad, what is it?
Potato salad, you know, and things like that.
They made a table and then we would sell it.
And we participated in that, too.
You know?
Where did people get flour and food?
Fun?
If you had money, you could get it.
For money, [LAUGHS] you can get it.
I don't remember name.
But we knew people and I guess we knew where to go.
I don't remember anymore how we got it.
But we had it.
And we-- it wasn't every Sunday, but every so often, we had those that had the money, they collected-- and came for the people who needed the money.
There was?
I am sure there was-- I don't remember now.
I don't remember.
I don't want to give you--
I'm sure there were.
But I don't remember that we went.
I really don't remember.
By whom?
By the guards in the ghetto.
By the Gentiles?
By the guards, by the SS.
Oh, by the guards.
No, it wasn't-- it wasn't bad.
Not, that I remember.
But when they-- it was the aussiedlung, the time that we had to go every so often, they would take people and send them away.
They would collect them and send them away.
So we had to get down.
And, you know, and just wait for our good luck or where they will take us, and you know, whether take us to the left or the right.
And like I mentioned, there was a small room on our balcony there, there was in the back balcony.
And it was just for stuff to put away and keep.
And when the people from the neighborhood had to come, they had to come.
And they didn't have an apartment, didn't have where to be.
So anyhow, there was that one room and the-- on the-- on the balcony there.
So it was a big like a-- a really small one room for garbage, for stuff to throw away or to put away.
So they had to move in there.
They had to move in there.
And when there was a aussiedlung, when they had the people, everybody, it was usually they called everybody that lived there to be on the street.
And then they were taking-- they were taking away mostly there was time where they took old people or workers, depending, younger ones or so on.
And there was a young couple with a little girl.
And so anyhow, everybody had to go down.
But they couldn't-- the husband couldn't go down because he broke his leg, and he couldn't walk.
So he stayed there in that room.
And she and the girl went back and went out.
And when she came back with the girl, the man was still in bed, but he was shot dead.
So the police came and checked out.
And they saw the guy in bed, and he was supposed to be outside so they shot him.
And that was it.
How did people react to what was going on?
We had to accept it.
We had to accept it.
What do we have to do?
We didn't accept it, they will shot.
So we tried to get away.
Not, no, not really personally I saw it.
There were lots of things which we heard about it.
And-- and there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but you-- we tried to stay away from all those things.
I don't know what to tell you.
Well, it was important to have a job.
So actually I got a job then, too.
And-- and it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there, where my husband worked, that was the lumberyard.
With my husband, I went there.
And I worked in the office.
You know, you tried to adjust [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to working.
That was the main thing, to have a job and to work.
Hmm?
